text: The special equipment, again there are surgical clips that are 5 mm.
Cautery, monopolar works, but the device you are seeing right now is the ligature and this is the device which I think especially for pulmonary resections is key.
This device is a variation on bipolar technology using radio frequency technology where it makes a seal without dividing the vessel.
But I will tell you that the new generation of the ligature, the failure rate for the seals is almost zero.

and again as I mentioned we put the patient in a lateral decubitus position and here is just an example of using an endo loop to do a lung biopsy so this is a think about a nine month old with interstitial lung disease of unknown diagnosis this is a PDS endo loop and I simply just snare the piece of lung that I want to do a biopsy on using the endo loop and the endo loop again is just a pre-tied suture ligature you can make your own if you want
and we snare it down and I'll put two of these in sequence

at the base of the lung and then I'll divide the lung distal to the ligatures and we get an excellent specimen this is actually a much better specimen than you would get trying to do an open lung biopsy through a mini thoracotomy because again you see there's very manipulation of the lung I just grab it in one place there's no tearing of the lung stretching and we get a very nice big specimen the other nice thing is that this this ligature is air and water tight
It means that it is as secure as a staple line.
At the end of the procedure, and I'll talk about this more this afternoon, we don't leave a chest tube in.
We simply inflate the lung, make sure there's not an air leak, and then we go ahead and pull the tube when we're sure there's not an air leak before we extubate the child and wake them up.
That's because the thing that hurts the most about a thoracoscopic lung biopsy is having the chest tube in afterwards.
The patient breathes much better and we've done that and had basically zero complication rate from doing that.
This is, I think, the biggest key.
When you're dealing with a pulmonary vessel in the chest, the key is to have vascular control.
Again, I told you I like using the ligature to do this because I feel very confident in the seals it promotes.
but here we are dealing with the main pulmonary artery to the right upper lobe
especially in larger children, and then I'll seal each one of the segments separately.
And that gives me proximal and distal control because I have more length of the vessel, I have more length of the main trunk, and so if the seal fails, I have an opportunity to recover.
And so here you can see I'm going to make a number of seals on the segmental vessels first.

and you can see again this seals the vessel without dividing it and I think that's key because if a device seals and divides at the same time and the seal fails then the vessel retracts and immediately you can't see because of the blood and you have no opportunity to recover and this way we'll make a number of seals so we have a seal on this main trunk and we have a seal on the segmental vessels and we have a gap in between
You can do this with sutures.
You can also do it with clips.
I don't like using clips because I'm afraid I'll knock them off during the surgery.
To do it with sutures can be somewhat tedious.
It certainly takes more time.
Plus you need two hands to do the suturing so you need another port to retract the lung for you.
Here I'm retracting for myself as I dissect.
You can see I'm just starting to cut across between the seals.
If there was some bleeding I would have an opportunity to put the ligature back in
or put a clip in or do something to stop it because the vessels don't retract and disappear.
So I think that's the safest way to take a pulmonary vessel.
